answers:
**1. Where new refund calls should live, and where the current code diverges.**
New provider calls (a `refund(...)` accessor) should live in `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py`. That is the documented single integration point: its docstring says "This module is the single place the chargekit provider client is built. All calls to the payment provider go through the accessors below" (`gateway.py:1-5`), and `ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7` states every provider call goes through `gateway.py`, with `errors.py` the only exception.

The code does **not** follow that pattern everywhere today. The `grep` shows `chargekit.Client(...)` is constructed in three files, only one of which is the gateway. Places that talk to the provider outside the gateway:
- `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py:14-16` — `charge_invoice` builds its own `chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY")` and calls `client.charge(...)` directly.
- `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py:9-13` — `run_dunning_pass` builds its own `chargekit.Client(...).charge(...)` directly.

(`errors.py:6` also imports from `chargekit`, but only the error classes — that is the sanctioned boundary exception per `errors.py:1-5` and `ARCHITECTURE.md:6-7`, not a divergence.)

Implication for the feature: adding `refund()` only to the gateway will not, by itself, guarantee refunds go through it — the two existing bypasses show the pattern isn't enforced, so a refund call could just as easily be inlined. These two call sites should be routed through the gateway (and ideally guarded) before or alongside the refund work.

**2. `ARCHITECTURE.md` vs. actual code.**
- *Payment integration boundary (`ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7`).* Claims every provider call goes through `gateway.py` with `errors.py` the only exception. This no longer matches: `api/invoices.py:14` and `workers/dunning.py:9` both instantiate `chargekit.Client` and call the provider directly. There are two undocumented boundary violations, not zero.
- *Invoice API response (`ARCHITECTURE.md:11-12`).* Claims the response contains "exactly" five fields: `id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status`. The actual serializer `InvoiceOut` (`models/invoice.py:16-28`) returns a **sixth** field, `external_ref`, which `charge_invoice` populates (`invoices.py:17`). So "exactly" is wrong — the response has six fields.

**3. What should happen to `ARCHITECTURE.md`, and when.**
The document has drifted from the code, so it should be corrected — but the direction of the fix differs per item, and this should happen now, before refund code is written, since the refund design depends on these contracts:
- The payment-boundary section describes the *intended* architecture the refund work should uphold. Rather than weaken the doc to match the current bypasses, the preferred path is to fix the code (route `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` through the gateway) so the doc becomes true again; if that cleanup won't happen first, the doc should at minimum be updated to acknowledge the two current exceptions so it isn't misleading.
- The Invoice API section is simply stale and should be updated to include `external_ref` (and drop the word "exactly," or restate the precise field list).
- The webhook section should be corrected per item 4.
Doing this before writing refunds avoids building the feature against a contract the doc misrepresents, and keeps the "single gateway" invariant meaningful for the new `refund()` accessor.

**4. Webhook event-types claim.**
`ARCHITECTURE.md:15-17` claims the integration handles 4 webhook event types: `payment_succeeded, payment_failed, refund_created, dispute_opened`. The code in `webhooks.py:10-19` handles **5**: those four plus `payment_pending` (`webhooks.py:14-15`). So the count is wrong and one handled type is undocumented. I would restate it to match the code — "handles 5 webhook event types" and add `payment_pending` to the list (or, better, phrase it as a maintained list rather than a hard-coded count so future additions like a refund-specific event don't silently invalidate the doc again). Note the four named types are all genuinely present, so the claim is under-stated rather than false about any specific type.
report:
I have everything I need.

## Plan
I was picking up a feature to add tenant refunds through the existing payment provider in the `medibill` backend. Before any code is written, I set out to (a) locate where provider calls are supposed to live and find every place that actually talks to the provider, and (b) check whether `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`'s claims about the invoice API, the payment integration boundary, and webhook event types still match the code — so a new refund path is built on an accurate picture.

## Actions
- Ran `find` / `grep` across the repo to locate the payment-related project; identified `medibill` as the relevant backend (`medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py`, `.../api/invoices.py`, `.../api/webhooks.py`, `.../workers/dunning.py`, `.../models/invoice.py`, `.../billing/errors.py`).
- Read `medibill/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` — states the provider boundary (all calls via `gateway.py`, sole exception `errors.py`), the Invoice API response fields, and 4 webhook event types.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py` — the sanctioned wrapper: builds one `chargekit.Client` and exposes `charge()` and `create_customer()`. No refund function today.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py` — the `POST /invoices/{id}/charge` route constructs its **own** `chargekit.Client` and calls `.charge()` directly, bypassing the gateway.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py` — `run_dunning_pass()` also constructs its **own** `chargekit.Client(...).charge(...)` directly, bypassing the gateway.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py` — handles 5 event types (adds `payment_pending`).
- Read `medibill/backend/app/models/invoice.py` — `Invoice`/`InvoiceOut` both carry an `external_ref` field beyond the documented five.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/billing/errors.py` — re-exports provider exception classes; the documented boundary exception.
- Ran `grep -n chargekit` across `medibill` to confirm the complete set of provider touchpoints.
- I consulted no external guides, playbooks, or reference documents; the `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` under review and `medibill/README.md` are the only docs I read.

## Answers

**1. Where new refund calls should live, and where the current code diverges.**
New provider calls (a `refund(...)` accessor) should live in `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py`. That is the documented single integration point: its docstring says "This module is the single place the chargekit provider client is built. All calls to the payment provider go through the accessors below" (`gateway.py:1-5`), and `ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7` states every provider call goes through `gateway.py`, with `errors.py` the only exception.

The code does **not** follow that pattern everywhere today. The `grep` shows `chargekit.Client(...)` is constructed in three files, only one of which is the gateway. Places that talk to the provider outside the gateway:
- `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py:14-16` — `charge_invoice` builds its own `chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY")` and calls `client.charge(...)` directly.
- `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py:9-13` — `run_dunning_pass` builds its own `chargekit.Client(...).charge(...)` directly.

(`errors.py:6` also imports from `chargekit`, but only the error classes — that is the sanctioned boundary exception per `errors.py:1-5` and `ARCHITECTURE.md:6-7`, not a divergence.)

Implication for the feature: adding `refund()` only to the gateway will not, by itself, guarantee refunds go through it — the two existing bypasses show the pattern isn't enforced, so a refund call could just as easily be inlined. These two call sites should be routed through the gateway (and ideally guarded) before or alongside the refund work.

**2. `ARCHITECTURE.md` vs. actual code.**
- *Payment integration boundary (`ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7`).* Claims every provider call goes through `gateway.py` with `errors.py` the only exception. This no longer matches: `api/invoices.py:14` and `workers/dunning.py:9` both instantiate `chargekit.Client` and call the provider directly. There are two undocumented boundary violations, not zero.
- *Invoice API response (`ARCHITECTURE.md:11-12`).* Claims the response contains "exactly" five fields: `id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status`. The actual serializer `InvoiceOut` (`models/invoice.py:16-28`) returns a **sixth** field, `external_ref`, which `charge_invoice` populates (`invoices.py:17`). So "exactly" is wrong — the response has six fields.

**3. What should happen to `ARCHITECTURE.md`, and when.**
The document has drifted from the code, so it should be corrected — but the direction of the fix differs per item, and this should happen now, before refund code is written, since the refund design depends on these contracts:
- The payment-boundary section describes the *intended* architecture the refund work should uphold. Rather than weaken the doc to match the current bypasses, the preferred path is to fix the code (route `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` through the gateway) so the doc becomes true again; if that cleanup won't happen first, the doc should at minimum be updated to acknowledge the two current exceptions so it isn't misleading.
- The Invoice API section is simply stale and should be updated to include `external_ref` (and drop the word "exactly," or restate the precise field list).
- The webhook section should be corrected per item 4.
Doing this before writing refunds avoids building the feature against a contract the doc misrepresents, and keeps the "single gateway" invariant meaningful for the new `refund()` accessor.

**4. Webhook event-types claim.**
`ARCHITECTURE.md:15-17` claims the integration handles 4 webhook event types: `payment_succeeded, payment_failed, refund_created, dispute_opened`. The code in `webhooks.py:10-19` handles **5**: those four plus `payment_pending` (`webhooks.py:14-15`). So the count is wrong and one handled type is undocumented. I would restate it to match the code — "handles 5 webhook event types" and add `payment_pending` to the list (or, better, phrase it as a maintained list rather than a hard-coded count so future additions like a refund-specific event don't silently invalidate the doc again). Note the four named types are all genuinely present, so the claim is under-stated rather than false about any specific type.

## Next steps
If allowed to change code, I would: (1) add a `refund()` accessor to `gateway.py` wrapping `_client`; (2) refactor `api/invoices.py` and `workers/dunning.py` to call the gateway instead of constructing their own `chargekit.Client`, restoring the single-boundary invariant; (3) update `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` — include `external_ref` in the Invoice API fields, correct the webhook list to 5 (including `payment_pending`) and add whatever refund webhook the provider emits, and keep the boundary statement accurate once the bypasses are removed; (4) check whether refunds need a new webhook type and/or an invoice status/field, and whether tests (e.g. `test_tracked_calls_guard`-style guards seen in the sibling `clinic-notes` project) should enforce "provider calls only in gateway."

## Confidence and gaps
High confidence on all four answers — each rests on files I read directly and a repo-wide `grep` for `chargekit` that enumerated every provider touchpoint (`gateway.py`, `errors.py`, `invoices.py`, `dunning.py`). Gaps: I did not inspect the `chargekit` SDK itself, so I can't confirm it exposes a `refund` method or which webhook event a refund emits. `load_invoice`/`list_overdue` are stubs (`models/invoice.py:31-37`), so runtime behavior of the charge/dunning paths is not exercised. I reviewed only static code, not tests or a running server, so I can't confirm the webhook route is actually wired into the app or that no other module reaches the provider through an indirection `grep` wouldn't catch (none appeared).
